label: Renewals & Transfers
title: Mortgage Renewals & Transfers in BC
metaTitle: Mortgage Renewals & Transfers — Greater Vancouver Broker
metaDescription: Don't auto-sign your renewal letter. Re-shop your mortgage at renewal — typically saves Greater Vancouver homeowners thousands over a 5-year term. No-cost transfer options.
intro: Your renewal letter is not a quote — it's an opening offer. Re-shopping your mortgage takes about 30 minutes and routinely saves my clients $5,000–$25,000 over a 5-year term.
body:
The Bank of Canada estimates that about 60% of Canadian mortgage holders simply sign whatever rate their existing lender mails them at renewal. That convenience costs the average Greater Vancouver homeowner thousands of dollars per term — because lenders price renewal letters above the rate they'll offer you when you push back, and well above what they'll offer a brand-new client.

The renewal window is also the cheapest moment to switch lenders. On a straight switch (same balance, no extension, no equity take-out), the new lender almost always covers your discharge fee, legal fees, and appraisal. Out-of-pocket cost: usually zero.

If your renewal date is within 120 days, this is the most leverage you'll have for the next five years. Use it.

highlights.title: $0 to switch lenders
highlights.description: On a straight transfer, the new lender covers discharge and legal fees in most cases.

highlights.title: Re-shop early
highlights.description: Most lenders hold a renewal rate for 90 days. Lock in early; you can usually still take a lower rate if it appears.

highlights.title: Renegotiate, don't just renew
highlights.description: Even staying with your current lender, a broker push-back typically drops your rate by 15–30 bps.

faqs.question: When should I start shopping my renewal?
faqs.answer: 120 days before maturity is ideal. Most lenders will hold a new rate for 90 days, so locking early protects you against rate increases without locking you out of better rates if they appear.

faqs.question: Is a transfer the same as a refinance?
faqs.answer: No. A transfer (or 'switch') keeps your balance, amortization and structure the same — you just move it to a new lender at a new rate. A refinance changes one or more of those things and triggers different rules and costs.

faqs.question: What if I'm with a private or B-lender?
faqs.answer: Renewals from private and B-lenders are where the biggest savings often live. Many clients can graduate back to A-lender pricing at renewal once their credit, income or property situation has stabilized.
